title: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
oneLiner: Dilated left ventricle with systolic dysfunction; common cause of heart failure in young/middle-aged adults; high risk of arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF) — thunderclap syncope or cardiac arrest in a stable patient:** ECG showing broad-complex tachycardia (VT) or polymorphic VT. **Do not delay defibrillation.**  
- **Acute pulmonary oedema with cardiogenic shock — cold, clammy, hypotensive, crackles throughout:** Single finding: cool peripheries + hypotension (SBP < 90) despite adequate filling. This is not just heart failure; it’s pump failure needing urgent inotropic support.  
- **Cardiac tamponade — muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins, pulsus paradoxus:** Bedside echo shows pericardial effusion with RV diastolic collapse. **Do not give diuretics – pericardiocentesis first.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea  
  - Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance  
  - Palpitations (atrial fibrillation, ventricular ectopics)  
  - Non-specific chest discomfort (rarely classic angina)  
  - Peripheral oedema (ankle, sacral)  
  - Nocturnal cough (due to pulmonary congestion)  
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Displaced apex beat (laterally, inferolaterally)  
  - Third heart sound (S3 gallop)  
  - Elevated jugular venous pressure (JVP) ± hepatojugular reflux  
  - Bibasal crackles (pulmonary congestion)  
  - Hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral pitting oedema (right-sided failure)  
  - Low volume, thready pulse ± irregularly irregular (AF)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Ischaemic cardiomyopathy:** history of MI, angina, ECG Q waves, coronary angiography shows >70% stenosis  
- **Hypertensive heart disease:** long-standing hypertension, LV hypertrophy on ECG (voltage criteria), diastolic dysfunction more prominent  
- **Valvular heart disease (aortic regurgitation, mitral regurgitation):** typical murmurs, echo shows valve lesion as primary cause  
- **Infiltrative cardiomyopathy (amyloidosis, sarcoidosis):** LV wall thickening on echo (not just cavity dilatation), QRS low voltage on ECG, multiorgan involvement  
- **Peripartum cardiomyopathy:** heart failure in last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum  
- **Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy:** prolonged uncontrolled tachyarrhythmia (e.g. AF, atrial flutter) – rate control often improves LV function  
- **Alcoholic cardiomyopathy:** heavy alcohol intake history, often Korsakoff syndrome, macrocytosis  
- **Chemotherapy-related (e.g. doxorubicin):** cumulative dose + recent chemotherapy  
**Splitter:** ECG showing pathological Q waves + wall motion abnormality on echo = ischaemic (CAD) vs. no Q waves + global hypokinesia = idiopathic DCM. Perform angiography.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - 12-lead ECG: assess rhythm, QRS width, Q waves, LV hypertrophy, low voltage  
  - Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio > 0.5), pulmonary congestion, pleural effusion  
  - Arterial blood gas (ABG) or venous (VBG): lactate, pH, pCO2 (to rule out acidosis)  
  - Capillary refill time, pulse oximetry  
  - Bedside echo (if available) – look for LV cavity dilatation, systolic dysfunction, valvular lesions, pericardial effusion  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Full blood count (anaemia)  
  - Urea, creatinine, eGFR, electrolytes (K+, Na+)  
  - Liver function tests (congestive hepatopathy)  
  - High-sensitivity troponin (to rule out MI)  
  - NT-proBNP / BNP (elevated in heart failure; rule-out threshold < 125 pg/mL)  
  - Thyroid function (thyrotoxicosis/hypothyroidism)  
  - Iron studies + ferritin (iron deficiency common)  
  - HIV serology + viral hepatitis serology  
  - Autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-Ro, anti-La if peripartum)  
  - Coeliac serology (anti-tTG) – DCM association  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) – first line: measure LVEF, LV end-diastolic diameter, wall motion, valve function  
  - Cardiac MRI – second line for aetiology (myocarditis, infiltration, fibrosis)  
  - CT coronary angiogram – if ischaemia suspected but coronary anatomy unclear  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Ambulatory ECG (Holter or loop recorder) – for ventricular arrhythmias, AF burden  
  - Endomyocardial biopsy – very rarely; only if giant cell myocarditis or sarcoidosis suspected and treatable  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Reduced LVEF (< 40%) + LV end-diastolic diameter > 112% of predicted (corrected for BSA/age)  
- **Never-miss test:** 12-lead ECG – missed VT/VF is fatal; also check for bundle branch block (ICD/CRT eligibility)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If pulmonary oedema with SBP > 90: oxygen + IV furosemide 40-80 mg + sublingual GTN if SBP > 110  
  - If cardiogenic shock (SBP < 90, cool peripheries): **do not use nitrates**; IV dobutamine (5-20 mcg/kg/min) or noradrenaline to target MAP > 65; consider non-invasive ventilation (CPAP)  
  - If VT/VF: defibrillate (monophasic 360J/biphasic 200J), IV amiodarone 300 mg bolus  
  - If extreme tachycardia (e.g. AF > 180 bpm) causing LV dysfunction: rate control with IV beta-blocker (e.g. metoprolol 5 mg IV) or amiodarone if hypotensive – but **do not rate-control in cardiogenic shock**  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start or re-titrate disease-modifying therapy (GDMT) once haemodynamically stable (SBP > 100, no renal impairment):  
    - ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 2.5 mg od) OR ARB (e.g. candesartan 4 mg od)  
    - Beta-blocker (e.g. bisoprolol 1.25 mg od or carvedilol 3.125 mg bd) – start low, up-titrate slowly  
    - Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) – e.g. spironolactone 25 mg od (if eGFR > 30, K+ < 5.0)  
    - SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10 mg od or empagliflozin 10 mg od) – reduce risk of hospitalisation  
  - Fluid balance chart, daily weight, strict input/output  
  - Diurese with furosemide if fluid overloaded (e.g. 40-80 mg PO/IV, titrated to weight loss 1-2 kg/day)  
  - Consider device therapy: if EF ≤ 35% after 3 months optimal therapy, refer for ICD ± CRT (if QRS ≥ 150 ms, LBBB)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Titrate GDMT to maximum tolerated doses over 2-3 months  
  - Lifestyle: limit sodium < 2g/day, reduce alcohol ≤ 14 units/week (aim for abstinence if alcoholic cardiomyopathy)  
  - Vaccinations: influenza annual, pneumococcal (once if > 65 or immunosuppressed, plus one dose pneumococcal conjugate)  
  - Address reversible causes: treat thyroid, cease cardiotoxic drugs, alcohol rehab, anaemia correction (IV iron if ferritin < 100)  
  - Consider rhythm control if AF with symptoms  
  - Anticoagulation if AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥ 1 in men, ≥ 2 in women) using DOAC (apixaban 5 mg bd) – check creatinine clearance  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluid restriction if oedema (1.5 L/day) – but not routine in all, individualise based on hyponatraemia  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if weight > 50 kg, renal function OK (consider if high bleed risk – give mechanical prophylaxis instead)  
  - Diet: low salt (no added salt meals)  
  - Observations: 4-6 hourly NEWS2 (heart rate, BP, oxygen saturations, respiratory rate, temperature)  
  - Daily weight and strict fluid balance
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Ramipril** (ACEi) – start 2.5 mg PO od, titrate to 5-10 mg od as tolerated (target BP > 100, eGFR stable)  
  - **Bisoprolol** (beta-blocker) – start 1.25 mg PO od, titrate every 2 weeks to 10 mg od (if heart rate > 60, BP > 100)  
  - **Spironolactone** (MRA) – start 25 mg PO od, increase to 50 mg od if K+ < 5.0, eGFR > 30  
  - **Dapagliflozin** (SGLT2i) – 10 mg PO od (no dose adjustment for renal function; eGFR ≥ 25 can start)  
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Candesartan (ARB) 4-32 mg od  
  - Valsartan 40-160 mg bd  
  - Carvedilol (beta-blocker) 3.125 mg bd → 25-50 mg bd  
  - Empagliflozin 10 mg od  
  - Ivabradine 5-7.5 mg bd (if unable to tolerate beta-blocker, or as add-on when HR > 70 despite beta-blocker)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - ACEi/ARB + MRA + SGLT2i = triple RAAS blockade not recommended (risk hyperkalaemia, renal dysfunction)  
  - Beta-blocker + verapamil/diltiazem – AV block risk  
  - Spironolactone + K+ supplements – avoid (check K+ before each titration)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACEi/ARB: reduce dose if creatinine > 200 µmol/L or eGFR < 30; withhold if creatinine rises > 30% or eGFR drops > 25% within 2 weeks  
  - MRA: avoid if eGFR < 30 or K+ > 5.0; start at 12.5 mg if eGFR 30-50  
  - SGLT2i: no dose adjustment for eGFR ≥ 25; caution in liver impairment (dapagliflozin not recommended in severe liver disease)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting beta-blocker (bisoprolol 5-10 mg) in acute decompensated heart failure with SBP < 100 – precipitates cardiogenic shock. Always start low and uptitrate when stable.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Acute pulmonary oedema – requires urgent diuresis ± NIV  
  - Cardiogenic shock – inotropes, consider MCS (e.g. IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO)  
  - Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF) – defibrillate, amiodarone, refer for ICD  
  - Thromboembolism – stroke from LV thrombus (especially if EF < 35% + AF)  
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive heart failure (NYHA III–IV)  
  - Sudden cardiac death (SCD) – leading cause of death in DCM  
  - Secondary mitral regurgitation (LV dilatation → annular stretch)  
  - Hepatorenal syndrome (right heart failure → chronic congestion)  
  - Atrial fibrillation (up to 30% prevalence)  
  - Malnutrition / cardiac cachexia (late stage)
sections.Prognosis:
- With optimal GDMT (ACEi + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i) + device therapy, 5-year survival ~50-60%.  
- Worse if: NYHA class IV, hyponatraemia (< 135), low SBP (< 100), high BNP / NT-proBNP, frequent NSVT, QRS prolonged, poor LVEF recovery (< 35% after 3 months).  
- Idiopathic DCM: ~25% show improvement in LVEF; others progress or need transplant.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always screen for iron deficiency** (ferritin < 100 or ferritin 100-300 with TSAT < 20%) – treat with IV ferric carboxymaltose if symptomatic heart failure despite GDMT.  
- **Check family history** of sudden death, cardiomyopathy – if positive, refer to cardiogenetics / cascade screening.  
- **Prophylactic ICD** if LVEF ≤ 35% after 3 months of optimal medical therapy and life expectancy > 1 year – reduces SCD mortality.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start beta-blockers in acute decompensation** – stabilise with diuretics/vasodilators first; beta-blockers can reduce cardiac output acutely.  
- **Do not forget to check for hyperkalaemia before starting MRA** – spironolactone can cause fatal arrhythmia if K+ > 5.5.  
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs** (including COX-2 inhibitors) – cause fluid retention, worsen heart failure, and reduce ACEi/ARB efficacy.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, palpitations, or syncope  
  - Weight gain > 2 kg in 2 days (or > 1 kg overnight)  
  - New leg swelling or ascites  
  - Fever/chills (risk of infection precipitating decompensation)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology clinic within 2-4 weeks for GDMT titration  
  - Echo at 3 months to reassess LVEF  
  - Device clinic if ICD/CRT planned
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / cardiac arrest team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially if rising trend)  
  - Sustained VT > 30 sec or syncope  
  - SBP < 90 mmHg unresponsive to fluid challenge (if dry) or to diuresis (if wet)  
  - Lactate > 2.0 mmol/L from venous/arterial gas  
  - GCS < 15 or new confusion  
  - Oxygen saturations < 92% despite maximum supplemental oxygen  
- **Consider ICU referral if:** need for inotropes, non-invasive ventilation failing, or multi-organ failure.

rawMarkdown:
## Dilated Cardiomyopathy

**One-liner:** Dilated left ventricle with systolic dysfunction; common cause of heart failure in young/middle-aged adults; high risk of arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF) — thunderclap syncope or cardiac arrest in a stable patient:** ECG showing broad-complex tachycardia (VT) or polymorphic VT. **Do not delay defibrillation.**  
- **Acute pulmonary oedema with cardiogenic shock — cold, clammy, hypotensive, crackles throughout:** Single finding: cool peripheries + hypotension (SBP < 90) despite adequate filling. This is not just heart failure; it’s pump failure needing urgent inotropic support.  
- **Cardiac tamponade — muffled heart sounds, distended neck veins, pulsus paradoxus:** Bedside echo shows pericardial effusion with RV diastolic collapse. **Do not give diuretics – pericardiocentesis first.**

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea  
  - Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance  
  - Palpitations (atrial fibrillation, ventricular ectopics)  
  - Non-specific chest discomfort (rarely classic angina)  
  - Peripheral oedema (ankle, sacral)  
  - Nocturnal cough (due to pulmonary congestion)  
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Displaced apex beat (laterally, inferolaterally)  
  - Third heart sound (S3 gallop)  
  - Elevated jugular venous pressure (JVP) ± hepatojugular reflux  
  - Bibasal crackles (pulmonary congestion)  
  - Hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral pitting oedema (right-sided failure)  
  - Low volume, thready pulse ± irregularly irregular (AF)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Ischaemic cardiomyopathy:** history of MI, angina, ECG Q waves, coronary angiography shows >70% stenosis  
- **Hypertensive heart disease:** long-standing hypertension, LV hypertrophy on ECG (voltage criteria), diastolic dysfunction more prominent  
- **Valvular heart disease (aortic regurgitation, mitral regurgitation):** typical murmurs, echo shows valve lesion as primary cause  
- **Infiltrative cardiomyopathy (amyloidosis, sarcoidosis):** LV wall thickening on echo (not just cavity dilatation), QRS low voltage on ECG, multiorgan involvement  
- **Peripartum cardiomyopathy:** heart failure in last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum  
- **Tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy:** prolonged uncontrolled tachyarrhythmia (e.g. AF, atrial flutter) – rate control often improves LV function  
- **Alcoholic cardiomyopathy:** heavy alcohol intake history, often Korsakoff syndrome, macrocytosis  
- **Chemotherapy-related (e.g. doxorubicin):** cumulative dose + recent chemotherapy  
**Splitter:** ECG showing pathological Q waves + wall motion abnormality on echo = ischaemic (CAD) vs. no Q waves + global hypokinesia = idiopathic DCM. Perform angiography.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - 12-lead ECG: assess rhythm, QRS width, Q waves, LV hypertrophy, low voltage  
  - Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio > 0.5), pulmonary congestion, pleural effusion  
  - Arterial blood gas (ABG) or venous (VBG): lactate, pH, pCO2 (to rule out acidosis)  
  - Capillary refill time, pulse oximetry  
  - Bedside echo (if available) – look for LV cavity dilatation, systolic dysfunction, valvular lesions, pericardial effusion  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Full blood count (anaemia)  
  - Urea, creatinine, eGFR, electrolytes (K+, Na+)  
  - Liver function tests (congestive hepatopathy)  
  - High-sensitivity troponin (to rule out MI)  
  - NT-proBNP / BNP (elevated in heart failure; rule-out threshold < 125 pg/mL)  
  - Thyroid function (thyrotoxicosis/hypothyroidism)  
  - Iron studies + ferritin (iron deficiency common)  
  - HIV serology + viral hepatitis serology  
  - Autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-Ro, anti-La if peripartum)  
  - Coeliac serology (anti-tTG) – DCM association  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) – first line: measure LVEF, LV end-diastolic diameter, wall motion, valve function  
  - Cardiac MRI – second line for aetiology (myocarditis, infiltration, fibrosis)  
  - CT coronary angiogram – if ischaemia suspected but coronary anatomy unclear  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Ambulatory ECG (Holter or loop recorder) – for ventricular arrhythmias, AF burden  
  - Endomyocardial biopsy – very rarely; only if giant cell myocarditis or sarcoidosis suspected and treatable  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Reduced LVEF (< 40%) + LV end-diastolic diameter > 112% of predicted (corrected for BSA/age)  
- **Never-miss test:** 12-lead ECG – missed VT/VF is fatal; also check for bundle branch block (ICD/CRT eligibility)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If pulmonary oedema with SBP > 90: oxygen + IV furosemide 40-80 mg + sublingual GTN if SBP > 110  
  - If cardiogenic shock (SBP < 90, cool peripheries): **do not use nitrates**; IV dobutamine (5-20 mcg/kg/min) or noradrenaline to target MAP > 65; consider non-invasive ventilation (CPAP)  
  - If VT/VF: defibrillate (monophasic 360J/biphasic 200J), IV amiodarone 300 mg bolus  
  - If extreme tachycardia (e.g. AF > 180 bpm) causing LV dysfunction: rate control with IV beta-blocker (e.g. metoprolol 5 mg IV) or amiodarone if hypotensive – but **do not rate-control in cardiogenic shock**  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start or re-titrate disease-modifying therapy (GDMT) once haemodynamically stable (SBP > 100, no renal impairment):  
    - ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 2.5 mg od) OR ARB (e.g. candesartan 4 mg od)  
    - Beta-blocker (e.g. bisoprolol 1.25 mg od or carvedilol 3.125 mg bd) – start low, up-titrate slowly  
    - Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) – e.g. spironolactone 25 mg od (if eGFR > 30, K+ < 5.0)  
    - SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin 10 mg od or empagliflozin 10 mg od) – reduce risk of hospitalisation  
  - Fluid balance chart, daily weight, strict input/output  
  - Diurese with furosemide if fluid overloaded (e.g. 40-80 mg PO/IV, titrated to weight loss 1-2 kg/day)  
  - Consider device therapy: if EF ≤ 35% after 3 months optimal therapy, refer for ICD ± CRT (if QRS ≥ 150 ms, LBBB)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Titrate GDMT to maximum tolerated doses over 2-3 months  
  - Lifestyle: limit sodium < 2g/day, reduce alcohol ≤ 14 units/week (aim for abstinence if alcoholic cardiomyopathy)  
  - Vaccinations: influenza annual, pneumococcal (once if > 65 or immunosuppressed, plus one dose pneumococcal conjugate)  
  - Address reversible causes: treat thyroid, cease cardiotoxic drugs, alcohol rehab, anaemia correction (IV iron if ferritin < 100)  
  - Consider rhythm control if AF with symptoms  
  - Anticoagulation if AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥ 1 in men, ≥ 2 in women) using DOAC (apixaban 5 mg bd) – check creatinine clearance  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluid restriction if oedema (1.5 L/day) – but not routine in all, individualise based on hyponatraemia  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if weight > 50 kg, renal function OK (consider if high bleed risk – give mechanical prophylaxis instead)  
  - Diet: low salt (no added salt meals)  
  - Observations: 4-6 hourly NEWS2 (heart rate, BP, oxygen saturations, respiratory rate, temperature)  
  - Daily weight and strict fluid balance

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Ramipril** (ACEi) – start 2.5 mg PO od, titrate to 5-10 mg od as tolerated (target BP > 100, eGFR stable)  
  - **Bisoprolol** (beta-blocker) – start 1.25 mg PO od, titrate every 2 weeks to 10 mg od (if heart rate > 60, BP > 100)  
  - **Spironolactone** (MRA) – start 25 mg PO od, increase to 50 mg od if K+ < 5.0, eGFR > 30  
  - **Dapagliflozin** (SGLT2i) – 10 mg PO od (no dose adjustment for renal function; eGFR ≥ 25 can start)  
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Candesartan (ARB) 4-32 mg od  
  - Valsartan 40-160 mg bd  
  - Carvedilol (beta-blocker) 3.125 mg bd → 25-50 mg bd  
  - Empagliflozin 10 mg od  
  - Ivabradine 5-7.5 mg bd (if unable to tolerate beta-blocker, or as add-on when HR > 70 despite beta-blocker)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - ACEi/ARB + MRA + SGLT2i = triple RAAS blockade not recommended (risk hyperkalaemia, renal dysfunction)  
  - Beta-blocker + verapamil/diltiazem – AV block risk  
  - Spironolactone + K+ supplements – avoid (check K+ before each titration)  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACEi/ARB: reduce dose if creatinine > 200 µmol/L or eGFR < 30; withhold if creatinine rises > 30% or eGFR drops > 25% within 2 weeks  
  - MRA: avoid if eGFR < 30 or K+ > 5.0; start at 12.5 mg if eGFR 30-50  
  - SGLT2i: no dose adjustment for eGFR ≥ 25; caution in liver impairment (dapagliflozin not recommended in severe liver disease)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting beta-blocker (bisoprolol 5-10 mg) in acute decompensated heart failure with SBP < 100 – precipitates cardiogenic shock. Always start low and uptitrate when stable.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Acute pulmonary oedema – requires urgent diuresis ± NIV  
  - Cardiogenic shock – inotropes, consider MCS (e.g. IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO)  
  - Ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF) – defibrillate, amiodarone, refer for ICD  
  - Thromboembolism – stroke from LV thrombus (especially if EF < 35% + AF)  
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive heart failure (NYHA III–IV)  
  - Sudden cardiac death (SCD) – leading cause of death in DCM  
  - Secondary mitral regurgitation (LV dilatation → annular stretch)  
  - Hepatorenal syndrome (right heart failure → chronic congestion)  
  - Atrial fibrillation (up to 30% prevalence)  
  - Malnutrition / cardiac cachexia (late stage)

### Prognosis
- With optimal GDMT (ACEi + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i) + device therapy, 5-year survival ~50-60%.  
- Worse if: NYHA class IV, hyponatraemia (< 135), low SBP (< 100), high BNP / NT-proBNP, frequent NSVT, QRS prolonged, poor LVEF recovery (< 35% after 3 months).  
- Idiopathic DCM: ~25% show improvement in LVEF; others progress or need transplant.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always screen for iron deficiency** (ferritin < 100 or ferritin 100-300 with TSAT < 20%) – treat with IV ferric carboxymaltose if symptomatic heart failure despite GDMT.  
- **Check family history** of sudden death, cardiomyopathy – if positive, refer to cardiogenetics / cascade screening.  
- **Prophylactic ICD** if LVEF ≤ 35% after 3 months of optimal medical therapy and life expectancy > 1 year – reduces SCD mortality.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start beta-blockers in acute decompensation** – stabilise with diuretics/vasodilators first; beta-blockers can reduce cardiac output acutely.  
- **Do not forget to check for hyperkalaemia before starting MRA** – spironolactone can cause fatal arrhythmia if K+ > 5.5.  
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs** (including COX-2 inhibitors) – cause fluid retention, worsen heart failure, and reduce ACEi/ARB efficacy.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening breathlessness, chest pain, palpitations, or syncope  
  - Weight gain > 2 kg in 2 days (or > 1 kg overnight)  
  - New leg swelling or ascites  
  - Fever/chills (risk of infection precipitating decompensation)  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Cardiology clinic within 2-4 weeks for GDMT titration  
  - Echo at 3 months to reassess LVEF  
  - Device clinic if ICD/CRT planned

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / cardiac arrest team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially if rising trend)  
  - Sustained VT > 30 sec or syncope  
  - SBP < 90 mmHg unresponsive to fluid challenge (if dry) or to diuresis (if wet)  
  - Lactate > 2.0 mmol/L from venous/arterial gas  
  - GCS < 15 or new confusion  
  - Oxygen saturations < 92% despite maximum supplemental oxygen  
- **Consider ICU referral if:** need for inotropes, non-invasive ventilation failing, or multi-organ failure.
